articles.title: Daily AI Briefing — Saturday, June 13, 2026 ET
articles.summary:
Daily AI Briefing — Saturday, June 13, 2026 ET

1. Today’s actual signal
- OpenAI Codex is pushing browser-agent debugging closer to real dev work: June 11 Codex added Developer Mode with controlled Chrome DevTools Protocol access, `/init` project instructions, and faster browser use. Source: https://developers.openai.com/codex/changelog — credible because it is OpenAI’s official Codex changelog.
- GitHub moved “agentic workflows” into public preview: natural-language Markdown workflows compile into GitHub Actions YAML, with read-only defaults, sandboxing, firewalling, safe outputs, and threat detection. Source: https://github.blog/changelog/2026-06-11-github-agentic-workflows-is-now-in-public-preview/ — credible because it is GitHub’s official changelog.
- Kilo Code is worth watching as the cheap/open coding-agent lane: open-source, VS Code/JetBrains/CLI/Slack/Cloud support, 500+ models, BYOK/local options, and “no markup” pricing claims. Sources: https://kilo.ai/ and https://github.com/Kilo-Org/kilocode — credible because they are the product site + public repo. Pricing still needs verification before relying on cloud/gateway spend.

2. Dustin relevance
- Hermes/agents: GitHub Agentic Workflows is basically “cron + agent + guardrails” inside Actions. Good pattern to borrow: Markdown intent → generated workflow → read-only/sandboxed first.
- Fanbase Weather: No strong signal today. Don’t duplicate the morning pipeline; if anything, borrow the GitHub guardrail pattern for approval-gated public-post experiments.
- Newsletter/Data Nerve: Strong angle: “The agent war is moving from chat UIs to controlled execution surfaces: DevTools, GitHub Actions, IDE agent windows, and auditable workflows.”
- App/product ideas: Small useful app: a local “agent run receipt” viewer for Hermes/Codex/Kilo that shows prompt, files touched, commands run, sources used, and approval status before anything public-facing happens.

3. Try / Save / Ignore
- Try today: Spend 20 minutes sketching one non-destructive GitHub Agentic Workflow for `dustin-ai-playground`: “summarize stale issues / broken links / failed builds,” read-only only, no auto-PR.
- Save for later: Kilo Code as the open-source/BYOK fallback when Copilot/Codex costs or limits get annoying.
- Ignore: Generic “AI coding agent rankings” posts unless they include pricing, repo activity, and actual workflow constraints.

4. Content angles
- “Agents are finally growing up: less ‘vibes in a chat box,’ more boring controls, logs, sandboxes, and budgets. That’s good news.”
- “The real product isn’t the model. It’s the harness: where the agent can act, what it can touch, and how you prove it didn’t do something dumb.”

5. One next action
- Create a short note titled “Agent workflow guardrails to steal” with 5 bullets: read-only default, sandbox, approval before writes, source links required, run receipt/audit log.

Verification: sources checked: OpenAI/Codex, Anthropic, xAI, Google AI/Developers, GitHub Copilot/GitHub Changelog, Hacker News, Product Hunt, GitHub/Kilo. Repetition check performed against local cron output; no matching prior AI briefing actions found, and no generic Fanbase Weather duplicate recommended. No external side effects.
articles.whyItMatters: This is the vetted Daily AI Briefing output created for Dustin, replacing the old generic AI RSS/news-card section.
articles.sourceLabel: Hermes Daily AI Brief · Jun 13, 5:06 AM ET

articles.title: Farmers RECC says no formal power application filed for proposed Barren County data center
articles.summary: WCLU Radio is reporting on Farmers RECC says no formal power application filed for proposed Barren County data center. In plain English, this is a large-load planning story: the important details are whether data centers are driving new power demand, who pays for the grid upgrades, and how regulators or lawmakers protect ordinary ratepayers. For Dustin, the useful read is whether this changes the local utility story around capacity, reliability, rates, regulation, metering, or the data products leaders need to explain those tradeoffs clearly. This is included in the morning brief because it is dated, source-linked, and matched the scan filters for this section.
articles.emailSummary: A data-center story with possible implications for power demand, grid upgrades, and who pays for capacity.
articles.whyItMatters: This is close to Dustin’s utility analytics world: it may affect metering, reliability, customer impact, rate context, regulatory reporting, or the data products leaders need to trust.

articles.title: Pennsylvania’s Power Bills Are Up 50 Percent Since 2020. The Reason Is the Data Centers.
articles.summary: The Eastern Herald is reporting on Pennsylvania’s Power Bills Are Up 50 Percent Since 2020. The Reason Is the Data Centers. In plain English, this is a customer-bill and rate-case story: the important details are what regulators approved, how much bills may change, what the utility says the money funds, and what protections or affordability terms are included. For Dustin, the useful read is whether data-center demand is starting to pressure utility forecasts, grid planning, rate design, or public communication around who pays for new infrastructure.
